event_summary: Iran unveiled a toll-charging mechanism for Hormuz shipping transit while the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing failed to produce a concrete plan to reopen the strait, leaving global energy markets disrupted by the ten-week-old blockade.
tldr: Across 28 buckets the headline corpus bifurcates between two beats: Iran's Hormuz toll mechanism (EXTERNAL_REGULATION, dominant in Qatar, Turkey, Vietnam, Iran state, UK) and the Trump-Xi summit's failure to produce a concrete reopening plan (POLITICAL, dominant in Canada, Israel, South Africa, Asia Pacific). Japan is the most isolated bucket (mean_similarity 0.39): Nikkei's single title frames the story as a domestic supply milestone — first LNG delivery since closure — rather than through any diplomatic or military lens. Syria's Enab Baladi ran an entirely off-topic headline about UN humanitarian progress in Syria. USA uniquely anchors its two titles in allied military capability failures (UK defense shortfalls, missing Indo-Pacific navies) rather than energy or deal-making.

frames.sub_frame: Hormuz toll mechanism and sovereign control

evidence.quote: Hormuz fallout responsibility lies with 'initiators of war'

isolation_top.note: Nikkei Asia's single title frames the story as Japan's domestic supply milestone (first LNG delivery since Hormuz closure) — the narrowest national-economy angle in the corpus, distant from all diplomatic and military titles.
Enab Baladi's headline covers Syria's UN humanitarian progress — entirely off the Hormuz story; the corpus carries no Syria-specific Hormuz-frame title.
Hungary Today frames the crisis through consumer-economy impact (kerosene supply and summer flight disruption) rather than the geopolitical and diplomatic frames dominant elsewhere.
News24 focuses on Trump's trade-deal optics and personal rapport with Xi — a political persona angle rather than Hormuz mechanics or energy economics.
AfricaNews grounds the story in Egyptian farmers' cost pressures — a human agricultural-impact angle at the most distal end of the supply chain, absent from all other buckets.

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Enab Baladi ran a headline on UN progress in Syria's humanitarian situation; the Hormuz closure's impact on Syrian food and fuel costs appeared only in the article body, not the title.
Middle East Eye's headline covered Israeli shelling in Khan Younis and Bureij refugee camp (idx 26) — off the Hormuz story; only The New Arab's headline was directly on-topic.
SCMP ran a headline about a Singapore deepfake Zoom scam tangentially tied to Hormuz; only Hong Kong Free Press covered the US-China summit with a Hormuz-relevant angle.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to frame the Hormuz story as a domestic supply-chain milestone — Japan receiving its first LNG shipment via the strait since the de facto closure — without any diplomatic or military framing.

Iran state newswire leads with a sovereignty counter-narrative — blaming 'initiators of war' and accusing the US of manufacturing false international support — framing Iran as defender rather than blockader.
Only outlet framing the Hormuz crisis as a structural failure of Indo-Pacific alliances — Asian navies largely absent from a waterway whose closure damages Asian economies most.
Both South Korean titles focused on the HMM Namu ship attack investigation and weapons debris analysis — a victim-state angle entirely invisible in all other buckets.
Only outlet grounding the Hormuz closure in agricultural livelihoods — Egyptian farmers squeezed by fertilizer and energy cost rises — the most distal human-cost angle in the corpus.

bottom_line: Headlines across 28 buckets split between Iran's Hormuz toll mechanism (the dominant EXTERNAL_REGULATION frame across 8 buckets) and the Trump-Xi summit's diplomatic stall (POLITICAL, 8 buckets), with Japan's domestic energy-supply angle the most isolated title in the corpus at mean_similarity 0.39. US outlets alone frame the crisis through allied military capability failures rather than deal-making or energy economics — a divergence that reflects Washington's unique vantage as the party bearing the military burden other partners are avoiding.
